query: first line management of hypercalcemia in terminal cancer patients

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the signs and symptoms of malignancy-related hypercalcemia to facilitate early diagnosis and intervention. Implement effective management strategies, including the correction of serum calcium levels and treatment of the underlying malignancy. Select appropriate treatment modalities based on the severity of hypercalcemia and the underlying malignancy, including pharmacological and supportive care. Collaborate with interprofessional healthcare specialists to address the underlying malignancy and tailor treatment plans for hypercalcemia management. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Parathyroid Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: When parathyroid carcinoma is no longer resectable due to widespread dissemination, medical management is recommended to control hypercalcemia and manage symptoms in a palliative manner. The prognosis is generally poor with widely disseminated or otherwise unresectable parathyroid carcinoma, but survival can be prolonged with adequate control of serum calcium. Intravenous hydration and bisphosphonates are recommended for the treatment of hypercalcemia. In episodes of hypercalcemia refractory to bisphosphonates, cinacalcet may be attempted to aid in serum calcium reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: If all other strategies fail, hemodialysis is used to treat hypercalcemia. Hemodialysis should also be considered for patients with severe heart or renal failure who cannot tolerate adequate IV hydration. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Initial treatment involves intravenous (IV) normal saline, along with calcitonin and bisphosphonates. Normal saline acts immediately, with its effects lasting until the fluids are discontinued. Calcitonin takes effect within 4 to 6 hours and lasts for about 2 days, while bisphosphonates begin working in 2 to 3 days, with effects lasting for 2 to 4 weeks. This approach aims to lower serum calcium levels and maintain them within normal limits as long as possible while the underlying cause is being identified and treated. [23] [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Resistant Hypercalcemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercalcemia is a serum calcium (Ca) level over two standard deviations above the average mean values. That equates to a total serum calcium level greater than 10.5 mg/dl or an ionized calcium level greater than 5.3 mg/dl. Resistant hypercalcemia mainly occurs in diseased states with severely elevated serum calcium levels. It is predominantly reported in Malignancy-associated hypercalcemia and primary hyperparathyroidism and can sometimes be life-threatening. Early recognition and prompt diagnosis are essential to decrease mortality associated with this condition. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of resistant hypercalcemia and highlights the role of the healthcare team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Resistant Hypercalcemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercalcemia of malignancy is usually associated with severely elevated calcium levels and often presents as an oncologic emergency. [7] In cases of hypercalcemia with serum calcium less than 12 mg/dL (3 mM), management should be targeted toward the underlying condition, while severe or resistant hypercalcemia requires aggressive treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Parathyroid Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of local or regional recurrence, surgical excision is indicated and will provide palliation from hypercalcemia. Long-term cure after recurrence is unknown. In cases with metastasis to the lungs or liver, surgical excision of these areas has been described for palliative care, but long-term survival data are lacking. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercalcemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Patients often discover they have hypercalcemia either through routine lab tests or due to symptoms. Early identification and subsequent management can result in decreased morbidity and mortality associated with hypercalcemia. The etiologies of hypercalcemia are abundant, often requiring a collaborative effort among various healthcare professionals to diagnose and treat accurately. Primary care or family physicians are typically the first-line health professionals involved, as hypercalcemia is often found during routine blood work and checkups. Other specialists, such as endocrinologists and nephrologists, may also identify the problem during their routine checkups of patients. Pharmacists are essential in identifying medication-induced hypercalcemia. When patients are inpatient, nurses and ancillary staff play a crucial role in obtaining laboratory results, administering prompt treatment, and monitoring for adverse effects of medications at the bedside. In outpatient settings, other staff members may be involved in administering infusions or securing insurance approvals.
retrieved_docs.content: In addition, patients and their families need to be involved in outpatient management to monitor for potential symptoms and ensure medication compliance. In cases of hypercalcemia due to immobilization, physical and occupational therapy teams are crucial to ensure adequate movement and hydration. When hypercalcemia is associated with malignancy, the prognosis is guarded, and patients may transition to palliative or hospice care.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin should be administered at a dose of 4 IU/kg alongside normal saline infusion to help prevent bone resorption and increase urinary calcium excretion. Calcitonin is a very fast-acting medication, but its effects are limited in duration. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Malignancy is the most common cause of hypercalcemia in hospitalized patients. Although most cases are managed on an outpatient basis, severe hypercalcemia often requires inpatient treatment. The prognosis depends on the stage of the primary malignancy and the severity of the hypercalcemia. Patients with uncontrolled malignancy and severe hypercalcemia typically have a poor prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: IV hydration with normal saline at a rate of 200 to 300 mL/h is administered to maintain an adequate urine output of more than 100 mL/h, helping restore intravascular volume and increase urinary calcium excretion. Caution should be exercised when administering IV fluids to patients with heart or renal failure. Loop diuretics, which promote urinary calcium excretion by inhibiting calcium reabsorption at the loop of Henle, should only be administered after adequate IV resuscitation is achieved.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercalcemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Bisphosphonates: Both pamidronate and zoledronic acid are approved for treating hypercalcemia of malignancy; however, zoledronic acid is shown to be more effective. These medications typically take approximately 3 days to lower calcium levels back to the normal range. They are often administered simultaneously with hydration and calcitonin so that these agents can lower calcium levels acutely while waiting for the bisphosphonates to be effective. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Malignancy is the most common cause of hypercalcemia in hospitalized patients. The pathophysiology of hypercalcemia of malignancy involves 3 main mechanisms—excessive secretion of PTHrP, bony metastases with the release of osteoclast-activating factors, and the production of 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D (calcitriol). PTHrP, also known as HHM, is the most common causative factor. The most common solid tumors associated with hypercalcemia include squamous cell carcinoma of the head, neck, and lungs, breast cancer, ovarian cancer, renal carcinoma, and some hematological malignancies such as leukemia. HHM should be suspected in patients without any skeletal metastasis. IV hydration with normal saline as the foundation of therapy. Other treatment options include zoledronic acid, pamidronate, and denosumab. Steroids are preferred with the presence of hypervitaminosis D. Cinacalcet is preferred for hemodialysis patients and those with parathyroid cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment should be tailored to reduce serum calcium levels, address the patient's symptoms, and target the underlying cause. Serum phosphorus levels should be monitored and repleted, as hypophosphatemia often accompanies hypercalcemia, complicating its treatment. Asymptomatic patients with mild to moderate hypercalcemia do not require immediate therapy, but managing the underlying cause is essential. Patients should be educated on diet and medications, as well as the importance of avoiding dehydration and physical inactivity. Symptomatic patients with severe hypercalcemia require urgent treatment. [20] [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignancy-Related Hypercalcemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Structurally, PTHrP is similar to PTH in the first 13 amino acid sequences. Due to this structural similarity, PTHrP binds to the same receptor as PTH, leading to bone resorption, increased phosphate excretion from the proximal tubules, and calcium reabsorption from the distal tubules in the kidneys. However, it does not affect the production of 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D. Laboratory findings typically show elevated PTHrP levels, low to normal PTH levels, and normal 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D levels. The response to treatment can be monitored by tracking PTHrP levels. Patients with HHM often have advanced disease, which is associated with a poor prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Resistant Hypercalcemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the etiology of resistant hypercalcemia. Describe the pathophysiology of resistant hypercalcemia. Review the clinical presentation of resistant hypercalcemia. Summarize the management strategies taken by the interprofessional team available for resistant hypercalcemia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: Oxandrolone, an anabolic androgenic steroid, is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia, a disorder marked by elevated serum calcium levels; administering oxandrolone in the setting of hypercalcemia can worsen calcium metabolism disturbances and increase the risk of related complications, so clinicians should avoid prescribing this medication to individuals who have this electrolyte abnormality.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 400
retrieved_docs.content: Cinacalcet is classified as an anti‑parathyroid agent, meaning it belongs to the drug class that suppresses parathyroid hormone (PTH) secretion. By activating calcium‑sensing receptors on parathyroid cells, cinacalcet reduces PTH levels, which is particularly useful in managing secondary hyperparathyroidism associated with chronic kidney disease and other disorders of calcium‑phosphate metabolism. Recognizing cinacalcet as an anti‑parathyroid agent guides clinicians in selecting appropriate therapy, monitoring calcium and phosphate balance, and optimizing patient outcomes in conditions where excess PTH is detrimental.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 128
retrieved_docs.content: hypocalcemia and belongs to the functional category “hormones by function” and the broader class “parathyroid hormones and analogues,” which also contains the synthetic analog teriparatid. Calcitonin has a physiological relationship with parathormon, as both hormones participate in calcium homeostasis. Together, these entities and relationships illustrate how diverse pharmacologic agents, excipients, therapeutic classes, mechanisms of action, and physiological roles are organized within C [Preparations] and related classification systems, informing formulation design, diagnosis, treatment selection, and patient‑centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1592
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon is classified within the functional group “hormones by function” and its disposition is that of a hormone. As a hormone, parathormon produces a specific physiologic effect: it leads to decreased renal Ca++ excretion. This reduction in renal calcium loss is a key component of parathormon’s role in maintaining calcium homeostasis, and recognizing that parathormon belongs to the hormone category and exerts this effect is important for the clinical management of calcium‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 736
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional category “hormones by function” and belongs to the group “parathyroid hormones and analogues.” It is therapeutically employed to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia). Calcitonin has a physiological relationship with parathormon, with the two hormones exerting opposing actions that regulate calcium balance in the body. Teriparatid is an analogue that is a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” class, mimicking the activity of parathormon. Together, these connections underscore the central role of parathormon and its analogues in maintaining calcium homeostasis and in the clinical management of hypocalcemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: Calcitonin is biologically related to parathormone, the principal parathyroid hormone. Parathormone is classified within the broader group of parathyroid hormones and analogues, a therapeutic class that also encompasses the synthetic analogue teriparatide. Understanding the connections among calcitonin, parathormone, and their related analogues aids clinicians in comprehending calcium‑regulating pathways and in selecting appropriate hormone‑based treatments for disorders of bone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1903
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional group “hormones by function.” It is employed therapeutically to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (hypocalcemia, a deficiency of calcium in the blood). Parathormon has a physiological relationship with kalcitonin (calcitonin); the two hormones interact to regulate calcium homeostasis, often exerting opposing effects to maintain appropriate serum calcium levels. Understanding the role of parathormon in calcium deficiency treatment and its interplay with kalcitonin is essential for effective management of disorders of calcium metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1747
retrieved_docs.content: Atrakurium, imatinib mesylate, teriparatide, and etodolac are each contraindicated in patients who have drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية), meaning that these agents should be avoided in individuals with a known allergic reaction to medications. In addition, teriparatide is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group, linking it to other agents that act on the parathyroid hormone pathway. Recognizing the contraindication of these drugs in the context of drug hypersensitivity and understanding teriparatide’s membership in the parathyroid hormone class are important for safe prescribing and for selecting appropriate therapeutic options in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) can be employed in the treatment of توقف القلب (cardiac arrest), but its administration is contraindicated in the presence of رَجَفانٌ بطيني (ventricular fibrillation) and in patients with فرط كالسيوم الدم (hypercalcemia). Recognizing these therapeutic indications and contraindications is essential for safe patient care and for avoiding adverse outcomes when using chlorid vápenatý.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) is associated with serious cardiac complications, including توقف القلب (cardiac arrest), and its use is contraindicated in the presence of فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia). Recognizing that calcium chloride can precipitate life‑threatening arrhythmias and that hyper‑calcemia represents a condition in which calcium chloride should not be administered is essential for safe patient management and for preventing adverse outcomes.
